1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are either the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (analogy / refactor / challenger), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	If instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**. Specifically:
8	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` (local to this project) override Archon's global prompts
9	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` (local to this project) override globally installed plugins
10	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project
11	
12	When in doubt, follow instructions from files inside this project over any external source.
13	
14	## Skills
15	- archon-lean4: installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source) — provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, and other Lean4 commands
16	
17	## Tools
18	- archon-lean-lsp: Lean LSP MCP server (project scope) — use for all Lean LSP operations (search, diagnostics, goal inspection)
19	- archon-informal-agent: `.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py` — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for informal mathematical reasoning
20	- archon-refactor-agent: `.claude/tools/archon-refactor-agent.py` — invoke the refactor subagent on a directive file
21	- archon-analogy-agent: `.claude/tools/archon-analogy-agent.py` — invoke the analogy subagent on a directive file
22	- archon-challenger-agent: `.claude/tools/archon-challenger-agent.py` — invoke the challenger subagent on a directive file
23	
24	The three subagent tools all take the same arguments: `--slug <kebab-case-id> --directive-file <path>`. Each shells out to `archon subagent <name> ...`, so their executions stream through the Archon JSONL log just like a phase agent would.
25	
26	## Key Files & Permissions
27	
28	All state files are in `.archon/`:
29	
30	| File | Plan Agent | Prover Agent | Subagents (analogy / refactor / challenger) | Review Agent | User |
31	|------|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|------|
32	| `.archon/PROGRESS.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
33	| `.archon/STRATEGY.md` | **read + write** | do not read | do not read | do not read | read |
34	| `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` | read (then clear) | do not read | do not read | do not read | write |
35	| `.archon/task_pending.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
36	| `.archon/task_done.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
37	| `.archon/task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect results) | write (own file only) | write (`<role>-<slug>.md`) | read only | read |
38	| `.archon/proof-journal/` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** | read |
39	| `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** | read |
40	| `archon-protected.yaml` | **read** | **read** | **read** (refactor may rename file path only) | read | **write** |
41	| `.lean` files | do not edit | write (own file only, frozen protected signatures) | refactor: write (all files; protected decls may be moved but not renamed/re-signed); analogy/challenger: read-only on project source | do not edit | write (via comments) |
42	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints, structure) | do not edit | do not edit | **write** (markers only: `\leanok`, `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections) | read |
43	| `Challenges/<Name>.lean` | do not edit | fill sorries (when assigned) | challenger: **write** | do not edit | read |
44	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | do not access | analogy: **write** | do not access | read |
45	
46	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow, run by hand by the mathematician.** The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. The plan agent invokes the refactor subagent by writing a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/` and passing its path to `archon-refactor-agent.py --directive-file`. This keeps each loop-driven refactor independently logged and avoids any chance of stale-directive reuse. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`, etc.) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise and prune them out as you rewrite.
47	
48	## Protected Declarations
49	
50	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Every agent must consult it before editing a `.lean` file. Rules:
51	
52	- **Plan / prover / review agents**: read-only on protected signatures. You may fill proof bodies, but not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
53	- **Refactor agent**: may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (keeping name + signature verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. Refactor agents may never rename, re-type, delete, or re-sign a protected declaration.
54	- The `archon-protected.yaml` file can only be edited by the user, no declaration can be added or removed by any agent. The only allowed modification is updating the file path of an existing protected declaration when its location was changed.
55	
56	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
57	
58	The blueprint uses two active markers.
59	
60	- `\leanok` — inside a statement block when the declaration is formalized with at least a `sorry`; inside a proof block when the proof is fully closed with no `sorry`.
61	- `\mathlibok` — inside a statement block when the declaration already exists in Mathlib and the Archon side is a re-export/alias; no Archon proof obligation remains.
62	- No marker — the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
63	
64	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between the prover and review phases each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves — let the script do it.
65	
66	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations remain the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
67	
68	## User Interaction
69	
70	Users provide hints in two places:
71	
72	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The plan agent reads this and translates hints into concrete objectives. Provers never read this file.
73	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
74	
75	## Agent Roles
76	
77	### Plan Agent
78	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for your full instructions
79	- Read `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` — incorporate hints, then clear them after acting
80	- Read `.archon/task_results/` — collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` and `task_done.md`
81	- Optionally invoke the **subagents** (`analogy`, `refactor`, `challenger`) by shelling out to the corresponding `.claude/tools/archon-<name>-agent.py` script via Bash, **before** writing prover objectives. See `.archon/prompts/plan.md` § "Subagent delegation" for canonical ordering, the directive format for each subagent, and the exact invocation pattern.
82	- Archive any subagent report you receive to `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<role>-<slug>-report.md` so the dashboard can render it.
83	- Write `.archon/PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round
84	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` (except for marker updates) and `\lean{...}` hints for the provers
85	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself
86	
87	### Prover Agent
88	- Read `.archon/PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it)
89	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/`:
90	  - autoformalize → `.archon/prompts/prover-autoformalize.md`
91	  - prover → `.archon/prompts/prover-prover.md`
92	  - polish → `.archon/prompts/prover-polish.md`
93	- Write results to `.archon/task_results/<your_file>.md`
94	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — **never edit another agent's file**
95	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your `.lean` file for file-specific hints
96	- **Do NOT edit blueprint chapters.** Marker updates are the review agent's responsibility. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker.
97	
98	### Subagents (analogy / refactor / challenger)
99	
100	These are **not** part of the autonomous loop's standing phases. The plan agent invokes them on demand by running the corresponding `.claude/tools/archon-<name>-agent.py` script via Bash, with `--slug <slug> --directive-file <path>`. Each subagent reads only the files the directive points it at — never `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, or other plan-agent state — and writes a self-contained report.
101	
102	- **`refactor`** — read `.archon/prompts/refactor.md`. Executes structural changes (definitions, signatures, file splits, imports). Inserts `sorry` at broken proof sites; never fills proofs. Writes report to `.archon/task_results/refactor-<slug>.md`.
103	- **`analogy`** — read `.archon/prompts/analogy.md`. Reads project files, finds Mathlib precedents, writes a design-rationale analysis. Persistent output at `analogies/<slug>.md`; report at `.archon/task_results/analogy-<slug>.md`. Read-only on project source.
104	- **`challenger`** — read `.archon/prompts/challenger.md`. Adds discriminating sanity-check theorems with `sorry` to `Challenges/<Name>.lean`. Provers fill them later. Report at `.archon/task_results/challenger-<slug>.md`. Read-only on target files.
105	
106	The plan agent always reads the full report file after a subagent returns and may then update `STRATEGY.md` / `PROGRESS.md` based on the findings. None of the subagents spawn other subagents.
107	
108	### Review Agent
109	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for your full instructions
110	- Read `.archon/proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data
111	- Write session journal to `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (summary.md, milestones.jsonl, recommendations.md)
112	- Update `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` with overall progress
113	- Maintain the **semantic** blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE: ...` annotations, stale `\notready` removal — in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch `\leanok`; it's handled by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase that ran before you.
114	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify PROGRESS.md
115	
116	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
117	
118	Two non-agent steps run automatically each iteration to reduce the burden on the agents above:
119	
120	- **Pre-compactors** (`compact-strategy`, `compact-task-pending`, `compact-task-done`, `compact-project-status`): run before plan / review, rewriting oversized state files in place while preserving every actionable detail. Configured under `compaction.*` in `.archon/config.json`. Skipped when files are below threshold.
121	- **`sync_leanok`**: runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status. Replaces what used to be a multi-page review-agent task.
122	
123	Both write inner-git commits (`archon[NNN/precompact/...]`, `archon[NNN/marker-sync]`) so their output is auditable and revertable.

1	# Refactor Agent
2	
3	You are the refactor agent. You modify definitions, signatures, types, and imports across all `.lean` files under plan agent direction. You can also create/delete/divide files under the plan agent's guidance.
4	
5	## Your Job
6	
7	The plan agent has identified a structural problem and written a directive describing what to change. Your invocation prompt tells you the directive's location (read it from disk) and the slug for this invocation. The directive contains:
8	- **Problem**: what's wrong
9	- **Mathematical justification**: why the change is correct
10	- **Changes requested**: exact replacements for each definition/signature
11	- **Affected files**: where cascading breakage will occur
12	- **Expected outcome**: what the sorry landscape should look like after
13	
14	Read the mathematical justification carefully — it tells you the intent behind each change, which you need when fixing cascading type mismatches in downstream files.
15	
16	## Slug
17	
18	Your invocation prompt contains a line `Slug: <slug>`. Use it for the report filename: `.archon/task_results/refactor-<slug>.md`. Multiple refactors per iteration are allowed and each uses a distinct slug, so do not write to the unsuffixed `task_results/refactor.md`.
19	
20	## Rules
21	
22	### Protected declarations
23	
24	Read `archon-protected.yaml` at the project root. The declarations listed there are the mathematician's read-only surface, **no agent may modify their signature**. As the refactor agent, under the directive you may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (keeping name + signature verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. You cannot do any other modification to `archon-protected.yaml`.
25	
26	### Blueprint-based informal content
27	
28	This project uses a blueprint (plasTeX + `leanblueprint`). Informal proof sketches live in `blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`, one file per Lean source file. The slug mapping is:
29	
30	```
31	Lean file  Algebra/WLocal.lean  →  chapter  blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex
32	Lean file  Core.lean            →  chapter  blueprint/src/chapters/Core.tex
33	```
34	
35	(The blueprint slug refers to the per-file chapter name, not the refactor invocation slug — the two are unrelated.)
36	
37	### What you CAN do
38	- Modify any `.lean` file: definitions, signatures, types, imports, module structure
39	- Create new `.lean` files or delete existing ones
40	- Delete false or wrong declarations
41	- Change quantifier ordering in lemma statements
42	- Add new definitions, structures, or type classes
43	- Insert `sorry` at proof sites broken by your changes
44	
45	### What you MUST do
46	- **Keep all files compiling.** After every change, check compilation with `lean_diagnostic_messages`. If a change breaks downstream proofs, insert `sorry` at the broken sites. The prover will fill them later.
47	- **Follow the plan agent's directive exactly.** Do not improvise beyond what was requested. If you think additional changes are needed, document them in the "Notes for Plan Agent" section of your report but do not make them.
48	- **Document every change** in `task_results/refactor-<slug>.md` (see Logging below).
49	- **Verify the full project compiles** before finishing. Use `lean_diagnostic_messages` on every file you touched plus files that import from them.
50	- **Ensure that the Lean files reflect the blueprint structure.** The plan agent gave you the directive and updated the blueprint with the intended structure. Your job is to make the necessary changes to the Lean files to match that structure.
51	
52	### What you MUST NOT do
53	- **Do NOT fill proofs.** If a proof breaks because you changed a definition, insert `sorry` and move on. Proof filling is the prover's job.
54	- **Do NOT edit PROGRESS.md, task_pending.md, task_done.md, or USER_HINTS.md.**
55	- **Do NOT make changes unrelated to the directive.**
56	- **Do NOT modify the names or signatures of protected declarations listed in `archon-protected.yaml`.** You may move them to a different file, but not rename or re-sign them.
57	- **Do NOT modify the blueprint chapters.** The plan agent updates the blueprint with the intended informal structure and markers; your job is only to make the Lean files match that structure.
58	- **Do NOT spawn other subagents.**
59	
60	## Workflow
61	
62	1. Read the directive file pointed to by your invocation prompt
63	2. Read the **Mathematical justification** section — understand why each change is correct
64	3. Read the blueprint chapters corresponding to the affected files to understand the intended structure and how the changes fit into it
65	4. Read the affected `.lean` files to understand the current state
66	5. Plan your changes: list which files need modification and in what order (modify definitions first, then fix downstream consumers)
67	6. Execute changes file by file, checking compilation after each file
68	7. Handle cascading breakage: when changing a definition in file A breaks file B, fix the type signatures in B and insert `sorry` at broken proofs
69	8. Verify compilation across all affected files
70	9. Write your report to `task_results/refactor-<slug>.md`
71	
72	## Handling Cascading Changes
73	
74	When you change a definition, expect downstream breakage. Handle it systematically:
75	
76	1. **Type mismatches:** Update signatures to match the new definition. Use the mathematical justification to determine the correct new types. This is your job.
77	2. **Broken proofs:** Insert `sorry`. This is the prover's job.
78	3. **Missing fields (if you changed a structure):** Add the new fields with `sorry` default values, or update construction sites.
79	4. **Import changes:** If you move or rename declarations, update imports in all affected files.
80	
81	## Logging
82	
83	Write your report to `task_results/refactor-<slug>.md` (where `<slug>` is the slug from your invocation prompt). This report is the primary communication channel back to the plan agent — be precise and thorough.
84	
85	```markdown
86	# Refactor Report
87	
88	## Slug
89	<slug>
90	
91	## Status
92	<COMPLETE or INCOMPLETE>
93	<If INCOMPLETE, explain exactly which changes could not be made and why.>
94	
95	## Directive
96	<Copy the Problem and Changes sections from the directive you received.>
97	
98	## Changes Made
99	
100	### File: <path>
101	- **What:** <description of change>
102	- **Why:** <from directive>
103	- **Cascading:** <list of files that broke and were fixed>
104	
105	### File: <path>
106	...
107	
108	## New Sorries Introduced
109	- `<file>:<line>` — <brief description of what proof broke and why>
110	- ...
111	
112	## Compilation Status
113	- <file>: compiles / errors (describe)
114	- ...
115	
116	## Notes for Plan Agent
117	<Anything the plan agent should know:
118	- Unexpected complications encountered
119	- Additional changes you think are needed but did NOT make (per the rules)
120	- Whether the mathematical justification was sufficient to guide cascading fixes
121	- Suggested follow-up refactors for the next iteration>
122	```
123	
124	## Return value
125	
126	Your final assistant message must be a concise summary, not the full report:
127	
128	- One line: `<slug>: COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE — <one-sentence outcome>`
129	- A bullet list of new sorry sites introduced (file:line)
130	- A bullet list of any divergence from the directive
131	- The path to your full report file
132	
133	The plan agent reads the full report file. Keep the inline return short — long inline returns inflate the parent's context.
134	
135	The **Status** field in the report is critical: if you write `INCOMPLETE`, the plan agent knows it may need to write another directive in the next iteration. If you write `COMPLETE`, the plan agent will proceed to assign provers to the new sorries.
136	
137	## Write Permissions
138	
139	| File | Permission |
140	|------|-----------|
141	| Any `.lean` file | **read + write** |
142	| `task_results/refactor-<slug>.md` | **write** |
143	| `archon-protected.yaml` | **read** (file path updates only when moving a protected decl) |
144	| All other state files | **read only** |

400	
401	This is the structural skeleton common to *every* `IsCechAcyclicCover` instance
402	proof: the actual mathematical content lives in establishing
403	`(cechCochain C F 𝒰).ExactAt n` for each `n > 0`. -/
404	theorem cechCohomology_subsingleton_of_cechCochain_exactAt
405	    {k : Type u} [Field k] {C : Over (Spec (.of k))}
406	    {F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat) (ModuleCat.{u} k)}
407	    {ι : Type u} {𝒰 : ι → TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat}
408	    {n : ℕ} (h : (cechCochain C F 𝒰).ExactAt n) :
409	    Subsingleton (cechCohomology C F 𝒰 n) :=
410	  ModuleCat.subsingleton_of_isZero h.isZero_homology
411	
412	/-- Iter-087 (refactor): per-summand R-linear restriction-collapse. For nested
413	opens `V ≤ W ≤ U` in a scheme `C.left`, the chain of presheaf restrictions
414	`Γ(U) → Γ(W) → Γ(V)` collapses to the direct restriction `Γ(U) → Γ(V)`.
415	
416	Used in the proof of R-linearity of the Čech differential
417	(`cechCofaceMap_pi_smul`): each per-summand restriction chain
418	`R = Γ(C.left, U) → Γ(C.left, V_{j∘δ_i}) → Γ(C.left, V_j)` collapses to the
419	direct map `R → Γ(C.left, V_j)`, matching the per-i R-action induced by the
420	restriction algebra structure on the cochain factor.
421	
422	Extracted iter-087 from the inline `have R_restrict_R_linear` (iter-086) inside
423	`basicOpenCover_isCechAcyclicCover_toModuleKSheaf`. The body is the
424	2-tactic proof verified by the iter-086 prover. -/
425	lemma presheafMap_restrict_collapse
426	    {k : Type u} [Field k] {C : Over (Spec (.of k))}
427	    {V W U : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat}
428	    (h_VW : V ≤ W) (h_VU : V ≤ U) (h_WU : W ≤ U)
429	    (r : C.left.presheaf.obj (Opposite.op U)) :
430	    (C.left.presheaf.map (homOfLE h_VW).op).hom
431	        ((C.left.presheaf.map (homOfLE h_WU).op).hom r) =
432	      (C.left.presheaf.map (homOfLE h_VU).op).hom r := by
433	  rw [← ConcreteCategory.comp_apply, ← C.left.presheaf.map_comp]
434	  congr 1
435	
436	/-- Iter-087 (refactor): R-linearity of a Čech-cochain differential at degree
437	`(prev n, n)` in the product representation.
438	
439	Given linear isomorphisms `e₁ : ↑(∏ᶜ Z₁) ≃ₗ[k] (∀ i, ↑(Z₁ i))` and
440	`e₂ : ↑(∏ᶜ Z₂) ≃ₗ[k] (∀ i, ↑(Z₂ i))` (typically the `ModuleCat.piIsoPi`-based
441	isomorphisms), R-module structures `h_mod_pi₁`, `h_mod_pi₂` on the dependent
442	products (typically `Pi.module` built from per-summand restriction-algebra
443	structures), and a morphism `scK₀_f : ∏ᶜ Z₁ ⟶ ∏ᶜ Z₂` (typically the Čech
444	differential `scK₀.f`), the morphism is R-linear when transported through
445	`e₁`/`e₂`:
446	  `e₂ (scK₀_f.hom (e₁.symm (r • y))) = r • e₂ (scK₀_f.hom (e₁.symm y))`.
447	
448	**Statement generality.** This theorem is stated abstractly in
449	`(Z₁, Z₂, e₁, e₂, scK₀_f, h_mod_pi₁, h_mod_pi₂, R)`. The mathematical content
450	(that the Čech differential is an alternating sum of presheaf restriction maps,
451	each of which is an R-algebra hom — see `presheafMap_restrict_collapse` — and
452	hence the sum is R-linear componentwise) depends on `scK₀_f` being the Čech
453	differential. The body is `sorry`; the iter-087+ prover filling it will either
454	specialize back to the project's Čech context (re-introducing the local
455	`scK₀`/`K₀`/`s₀`/`n` quantities and unfolding the differential as an
456	alternating sum) or add explicit hypotheses on `scK₀_f` (e.g. expressing
457	`scK₀_f` as `∑ i, (-1)^i • Pi.lift (...)` with each summand R-linear).
458	
459	The bulk of the closure roadmap is documented in iter-073→iter-086 of
460	`STRATEGY.md`; the key reduction step lives in `presheafMap_restrict_collapse`
461	above.
462	
463	Extracted iter-087 from the inline `have h_diff_pi_smul_f` inside
464	`basicOpenCover_isCechAcyclicCover_toModuleKSheaf` (which had accumulated
465	~250 lines of dead-end iteration scaffolding). -/
466	set_option maxHeartbeats 1600000 in
467	theorem cechCofaceMap_pi_smul
468	    {k R : Type u} [Field k] [CommRing R]
469	    {ι₁ ι₂ : Type u}
470	    {Z₁ : ι₁ → ModuleCat.{u} k} {Z₂ : ι₂ → ModuleCat.{u} k}
471	    (e₁ : (∏ᶜ Z₁ : ModuleCat.{u} k) ≃ₗ[k] (∀ i, Z₁ i))
472	    (e₂ : (∏ᶜ Z₂ : ModuleCat.{u} k) ≃ₗ[k] (∀ i, Z₂ i))
473	    (h_mod_pi₁ : Module R (∀ i, Z₁ i))
474	    (h_mod_pi₂ : Module R (∀ i, Z₂ i))
475	    (scK₀_f : (∏ᶜ Z₁ : ModuleCat.{u} k) ⟶ (∏ᶜ Z₂ : ModuleCat.{u} k))
476	    (r : R) (y : ∀ i, Z₁ i) :
477	    letI := h_mod_pi₁
478	    letI := h_mod_pi₂
479	    e₂ (⇑(ConcreteCategory.hom scK₀_f) (e₁.symm (r • y))) =
480	      r • e₂ (⇑(ConcreteCategory.hom scK₀_f) (e₁.symm y)) := by
481	  sorry
482	
483	-- Iter-078: lifted to 800000 because the iter-078 prover work on

1122	      -- localization target; the present formulation (using `LocalizedModule`
1123	      -- directly) sidesteps the product-localisation commutation argument and
1124	      -- is closable by typeclass synthesis.
1125	      --
1126	      -- Iter-071 prover: discharge `h_loc_X_i (f : ↑s₀)` for each `i ∈ {1, 2, 3}`
1127	      -- via `localizedModuleIsLocalizedModule` (kernel-only).
1128	      have h_loc_X₁ (f : ↑s₀) : IsLocalizedModule.Away (f.1 : R)
1129	          (LocalizedModule.mkLinearMap (Submonoid.powers (f.1 : R)) scK₀.X₁) :=
1130	        localizedModuleIsLocalizedModule _
1131	      have h_loc_X₂ (f : ↑s₀) : IsLocalizedModule.Away (f.1 : R)
1132	          (LocalizedModule.mkLinearMap (Submonoid.powers (f.1 : R)) scK₀.X₂) :=
1133	        localizedModuleIsLocalizedModule _
1134	      have h_loc_X₃ (f : ↑s₀) : IsLocalizedModule.Away (f.1 : R)
1135	          (LocalizedModule.mkLinearMap (Submonoid.powers (f.1 : R)) scK₀.X₃) :=
1136	        localizedModuleIsLocalizedModule _
1137	      -- Step 4: the localized Čech differential `LocalizedModule.map (powers f.1) f_R`
1138	      -- agrees with the slice-cover Čech differential (viewed as an `R`-linear map)
1139	      -- via the universal property of localization (`IsLocalizedModule.ext` plus
1140	      -- `map_comp`).  Consequently `h_a₀_fun f` yields exactness of the localized
1141	      -- differential at each `f ∈ ↑s₀`.
1142	      have h_loc_exact (f : ↑s₀) : Function.Exact
1143	          ⇑(LocalizedModule.map (Submonoid.powers (f.1 : R)) f_R)
1144	          ⇑(LocalizedModule.map (Submonoid.powers (f.1 : R)) g_R) := sorry
1145	      -- Step 5: apply `exact_of_isLocalized_span` to conclude global exactness of `K₀`.
1146	      rw [← hf_eq, ← hg_eq]
1147	      exact exact_of_localized_span (↑s₀ : Set R) h_top f_R g_R h_loc_exact

1149	    -- Close the original goal via the transport `K₀ exact → K exact` + exactness of `K₀`.

151	Given a Set `s ⊆ Γ(C.left, U)` and indices `i j : s`, the intersection of the
152	two basic-open cover members `basicOpenCover s i` and `basicOpenCover s j`

154	`Scheme.basicOpen_mul`.

198	
199	Given a non-empty `Finset t : Finset s`, the infimum (over `t`) of the basic-open
200	cover members `basicOpenCover s i` for `i ∈ t` equals the basic open of the

200	cover members `basicOpenCover s i` for `i ∈ t` equals the basic open of the
201	finite product `∏ i ∈ t, i.1` in `Γ(C.left, U)`. Generalises iter-056's binary
202	`basicOpenCover_inter_eq_basicOpen_mul` to arbitrary non-empty Finset indexing.
203	
204	Body via `Finset.cons_induction`: the empty case is impossible by `t.Nonempty`
205	(closed via `Finset.not_nonempty_empty`); the cons case splits on whether the
206	residual `t` is empty or non-empty (via `Finset.eq_empty_or_nonempty`). In the
207	empty residual case, `simp [basicOpenCover]` closes via single-element reduction
208	of `Finset.inf'` and `Finset.prod`. In the non-empty residual case, decompose
209	via `Finset.inf'_cons hne` to expose `basicOpenCover s a ⊓ rest`, decompose

211	`rest = C.left.basicOpen (∏ i ∈ t, i.1)`, then `change ... ⊓ _ = _` followed by
212	`rw [← Scheme.basicOpen_mul]` consolidates the product form.

215	at arbitrary degree `n`: the Čech cochain at degree `n` indexes `x : Fin (n+1) → s`
216	and assigns the sheaf evaluated at `⨅ i : Fin (n+1), basicOpenCover s (x i)`.
217	Via this lemma (instantiated at `t := (Finset.univ : Finset (Fin (n+1))).image x`,
218	or more directly via `Finset.univ.inf' Finset.univ_nonempty (basicOpenCover s ∘ x)`),
219	that intersection identifies with `C.left.basicOpen (∏ i : Fin (n+1), (x i).1)`,

227	      C.left.basicOpen (∏ i ∈ t, (i.1 : Γ(C.left, U))) := by

230	  | empty => exact absurd h Finset.not_nonempty_empty
231	  | cons a t ha ih =>
232	    rcases t.eq_empty_or_nonempty with rfl | hne
233	    · simp [basicOpenCover]
234	    · rw [Finset.inf'_cons hne, Finset.prod_cons, ih hne]
235	      change C.left.basicOpen (a.1 : Γ(C.left, U)) ⊓ _ = _

315	`algebra_section_section_basicOpen` instance (`Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Scheme.lean` L724)

323	This is the **fourth and final** thin foundational scaffolding helper on the affine
324	basic-open identification side. The iter-060+ basic-open Koszul acyclicity argument

326	substep 2) chains iter-057's equality + iter-058's inclusion + iter-059's localization

329	on localised modules. -/
330	theorem basicOpenCover_finset_inf'_isLocalization
331	    {k : Type u} [Field k] {C : Over (Spec (.of k))}
332	    {U : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat} (hU : IsAffineOpen U)

342	/-- Iter-066 helper: in `ModuleCat k`, a product projection induced by an
343	injection of index sets is a split epi.
344	
345	Given an injection `f : β → α` and a family `M : α → ModuleCat k`, the natural
346	projection `∏ᶜ_a M a ⟶ ∏ᶜ_b M (f b)` defined as `Pi.lift (fun b => Pi.π M (f b))`
347	(which sends a tuple indexed by `α` to its `image f` components, reindexed by `β`)
348	admits a section in `ModuleCat k`. The section is the "extend by zero" map:
349	given a tuple indexed by `β`, fill in the components in `image f` (using the
350	unique preimage given by injectivity of `f`) and set the remaining components
351	to `0`.
352	
353	This is the categorical fact underlying `h_π_split` in
354	`basicOpenCover_isCechAcyclicCover_toModuleKSheaf`: the cochain-complex map
355	`π : K ⟶ K₀` is at each degree of this form (the `↑s₀ ↪ s` inclusion induces a
356	postcomposition map `(Fin (i+1) → ↑s₀) → (Fin (i+1) → s)` which is injective by
357	injectivity of the inclusion, hence the induced product projection on cochain
358	factors is split).

360	The helper is stated for a generic injection so it can be reused for both the
361	`s` substep (`h_π_split` proper) and the slice-cover variants. -/
362	noncomputable def splitEpi_pi_lift_of_injective {k : Type u} [Field k]
363	    {α β : Type u} (M : α → ModuleCat.{u} k)
364	    (f : β → α) (hf : Function.Injective f) :
365	    SplitEpi (Pi.lift (fun b : β => Pi.π M (f b))) where
366	  section_ := by
367	    classical
368	    refine Pi.lift (fun a => ?_)
369	    by_cases h : ∃ b, f b = a
370	    · exact h.choose_spec ▸ Pi.π (fun b => M (f b)) h.choose
371	    · exact 0
372	  id := by
373	    classical
374	    apply Pi.hom_ext
375	    intro b
376	    rw [Category.assoc, Pi.lift_π, Category.id_comp, Pi.lift_π]
377	    have h : ∃ b', f b' = f b := ⟨b, rfl⟩
378	    rw [dif_pos h]
379	    -- The transport `h.choose_spec ▸ Pi.π _ h.choose = Pi.π _ b`.
380	    -- By injectivity of `f`, `h.choose = b`, so the transport collapses.
381	    -- We use the helper lemma below.
382	    have key : ∀ {b b' : β} (heq : f b' = f b),
383	        heq ▸ (Pi.π (fun b'' => M (f b'')) b' :
384	            (∏ᶜ fun b'' => M (f b'')) ⟶ M (f b')) =
385	          (Pi.π (fun b'' => M (f b'')) b :
386	            (∏ᶜ fun b'' => M (f b'')) ⟶ M (f b)) := by
387	      intro b b' heq
388	      have hbb : b' = b := hf heq
389	      subst hbb
390	      rfl
391	    exact key h.choose_spec

490	/-- Iter-060 (Phase A step 6 / Path 2 — **substantive Čech acyclicity attempt**):
491	the basic-open cover of an affine open `U` by a spanning subset `s ⊆ Γ(C.left, U)`
492	gives a Čech-acyclic cover for the `ModuleCat k`-valued structure sheaf
493	`toModuleKSheaf C`. This is the Stacks 01ED Čech-acyclicity theorem for affine
494	schemes in our setting.
495	
496	**Mathematical content (Stacks 01ED route).** For each positive degree `n`,
497	exactness of the Čech cochain at degree `n` is a *local* property on the
498	spanning set `s`. Localising the Čech complex at any `f ∈ s` exhibits the
499	slice over `D(f)` as a Čech complex of an affine cover whose terminal vertex
500	`D(f) → D(f)` supplies an *extra degeneracy* (Mathlib's
501	`FormalCoproduct.extraDegeneracyCech`). By
502	`SimplicialObject.Augmented.ExtraDegeneracy.homotopyEquiv` the augmented
503	chain complex of the slice is homotopy equivalent to the constant complex on
504	the augmentation, hence exact in positive degrees. Re-globalising via
505	`exact_of_isLocalized_span` (Mathlib `RingTheory/LocalProperties/Exactness.lean`
506	L173) yields exactness of the *unlocalised* Čech complex at degree `n`.

508	The structural skeleton is:
509	* reduce `Subsingleton (cechCohomology n)` to `ExactAt (cechCochain) n` via
510	  `cechCohomology_subsingleton_of_cechCochain_exactAt`;
511	* the substantive `ExactAt n` is decomposed into three substeps tracked by
512	  separate `sorry`s, each annotated with the missing computational step.

519	      `ExtraDegeneracy`, then transport via
520	      `ExtraDegeneracy.homotopyEquiv` and op-passage `CochainComplex.opEquivalence`
521	      to get cochain-level exactness in positive degrees;
522	  (b) **localised-Čech identification**: the localisation at `f` of the
523	      degree-`n` Čech cochain is the degree-`n` cochain of the cover-of-`D(f)`
524	      Čech complex, via iter-057+ identifications chained with

/-- Iter-087 (refactor): R-linearity of a Čech-cochain differential at degree
`(prev n, n)` in the product representation.

Given linear isomorphisms `e₁ : ↑(∏ᶜ Z₁) ≃ₗ[k] (∀ i, ↑(Z₁ i))` and
`e₂ : ↑(∏ᶜ Z₂) ≃ₗ[k] (∀ i, ↑(Z₂ i))` (typically the `ModuleCat.piIsoPi`-based
isomorphisms), R-module structures `h_mod_pi₁`, `h_mod_pi₂` on the dependent
products (typically `Pi.module` built from per-summand restriction-algebra
structures), and a morphism `scK₀_f : ∏ᶜ Z₁ ⟶ ∏ᶜ Z₂` (typically the Čech
differential `scK₀.f`), the morphism is R-linear when transported through
`e₁`/`e₂`:
  `e₂ (scK₀_f.hom (e₁.symm (r • y))) = r • e₂ (scK₀_f.hom (e₁.symm y))`.

**Statement generality.** This theorem is stated abstractly in
`(Z₁, Z₂, e₁, e₂, scK₀_f, h_mod_pi₁, h_mod_pi₂, R)`. The mathematical content
(that the Čech differential is an alternating sum of presheaf restriction maps,
each of which is an R-algebra hom — see `presheafMap_restrict_collapse` — and
hence the sum is R-linear componentwise) depends on `scK₀_f` being the Čech
differential. The body is `sorry`; the iter-087+ prover filling it will either
specialize back to the project's Čech context (re-introducing the local
`scK₀`/`K₀`/`s₀`/`n` quantities and unfolding the differential as an
alternating sum) or add explicit hypotheses on `scK₀_f` (e.g. expressing
`scK₀_f` as `∑ i, (-1)^i • Pi.lift (...)` with each summand R-linear).

The bulk of the closure roadmap is documented in iter-073→iter-086 of

a mathematically false statement for arbitrary `scK₀_f`. This corrective
version fixes `R`, `K₀`, `scK₀`, `Z_i`, `e_i`, `h_mod_pi_i` from the
Čech-cochain context, so the conclusion is mathematically true: the Čech
differential is a finite alternating sum of presheaf restriction maps, each
R-linear (see `presheafMap_restrict_collapse`), hence the sum is R-linear

theorem cechCofaceMap_pi_smul
    {k : Type u} [Field k] {C : Over (Spec (.of k))}
    {U : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat} (hU : IsAffineOpen U)
    (s₀ : Finset Γ(C.left, U)) {n : ℕ} (hn : 0 < n) :
    let R := Γ(C.left, U)
    let K₀ := cechCochain C (toModuleKSheaf C)
      (basicOpenCover (C := C) (U := U) (↑s₀ : Set Γ(C.left, U)))
    let scK₀ := HomologicalComplex.sc K₀ n
    let Z₁ := fun (i : Fin ((ComplexShape.up ℕ).prev n + 1) → ↑s₀) =>
      ModuleCat.of k (C.left.presheaf.obj
        (Opposite.op (∏ᶜ fun a => basicOpenCover (C := C) (U := U) ↑s₀ (i a))))
    let Z₂ := fun (i : Fin (n + 1) → ↑s₀) =>
      ModuleCat.of k (C.left.presheaf.obj
        (Opposite.op (∏ᶜ fun a => basicOpenCover (C := C) (U := U) ↑s₀ (i a))))
    let e₁ := (ModuleCat.piIsoPi Z₁).toLinearEquiv
    let e₂ := (ModuleCat.piIsoPi Z₂).toLinearEquiv
    letI perI₁ : ∀ i, Module R (Z₁ i) := fun i => by
      apply RingHom.toModule
      refine (C.left.presheaf.map (homOfLE ?_).op).hom
      let a0 : Fin ((ComplexShape.up ℕ).prev n + 1) := ⟨0, by omega⟩
      have h1 : ∏ᶜ (fun a => basicOpenCover (C := C) (U := U) ↑s₀ (i a)) ≤
        basicOpenCover (C := C) (U := U) ↑s₀ (i a0) := (Pi.π _ a0).le
      have h2 : basicOpenCover (C := C) (U := U) ↑s₀ (i a0) ≤ U :=
        basicOpen_le C.left (i a0 : Γ(C.left, U))
      exact h1.trans h2
    letI h_mod_pi₁ : Module R (∀ i, Z₁ i) := Pi.module _ _ _
    letI perI₂ : ∀ i, Module R (Z₂ i) := fun i => by
      apply RingHom.toModule
      refine (C.left.presheaf.map (homOfLE ?_).op).hom
      let a0 : Fin (n + 1) := ⟨0, by omega⟩
      have h1 : ∏ᶜ (fun a => basicOpenCover (C := C) (U := U) ↑s₀ (i a)) ≤
        basicOpenCover (C := C) (U := U) ↑s₀ (i a0) := (Pi.π _ a0).le
      have h2 : basicOpenCover (C := C) (U := U) ↑s₀ (i a0) ≤ U :=
        basicOpen_le C.left (i a0 : Γ(C.left, U))
      exact h1.trans h2
    letI h_mod_pi₂ : Module R (∀ i, Z₂ i) := Pi.module _ _ _
    ∀ (r : R) (y : ∀ i, Z₁ i),
      e₂ (⇑(ConcreteCategory.hom scK₀.f) (e₁.symm (r • y))) =
        r • e₂ (⇑(ConcreteCategory.hom scK₀.f) (e₁.symm y)) := by

example {k : Type u} [Field k] {C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))}
    {U : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat} (hU : IsAffineOpen U)
    (s₀ : Finset Γ(C.left, U)) {n : ℕ} (hn : 0 < n) :
    let R := Γ(C.left, U)
    let Z₁ := fun (i : Fin ((ComplexShape.up ℕ).prev n + 1) → ↑(s₀ : Set Γ(C.left, U))) =>
      ModuleCat.of k (C.left.presheaf.obj
        (Opposite.op (∏ᶜ fun a => C.left.basicOpen (i a : Γ(C.left, U)))))
    let Z₂ := fun (i : Fin (n + 1) → ↑(s₀ : Set Γ(C.left, U))) =>
      ModuleCat.of k (C.left.presheaf.obj
        (Opposite.op (∏ᶜ fun a => C.left.basicOpen (i a : Γ(C.left, U)))))
    let e₁ := (ModuleCat.piIsoPi Z₁).toLinearEquiv
    let e₂ := (ModuleCat.piIsoPi Z₂).toLinearEquiv
    letI perI₁ : ∀ i, Module R (Z₁ i) := fun i => by
      apply RingHom.toModule
      refine (C.left.presheaf.map (homOfLE ?_).op).hom
      let a0 : Fin ((ComplexShape.up ℕ).prev n + 1) := ⟨0, by omega⟩
      have h1 : ∏ᶜ (fun a => C.left.basicOpen (i a : Γ(C.left, U))) ≤
        C.left.basicOpen (i a0 : Γ(C.left, U)) := (Pi.π _ a0).le
      have h2 : C.left.basicOpen (i a0 : Γ(C.left, U)) ≤ U :=
        basicOpen_le C.left (i a0 : Γ(C.left, U))
      exact h1.trans h2
    letI h_mod_pi₁ : Module R (∀ i, Z₁ i) := Pi.module _ _ _
    letI perI₂ : ∀ i, Module R (Z₂ i) := fun i => by
      apply RingHom.toModule
      refine (C.left.presheaf.map (homOfLE ?_).op).hom
      let a0 : Fin (n + 1) := ⟨0, by omega⟩
      have h1 : ∏ᶜ (fun a => C.left.basicOpen (i a : Γ(C.left, U))) ≤
        C.left.basicOpen (i a0 : Γ(C.left, U)) := (Pi.π _ a0).le
      have h2 : C.left.basicOpen (i a0 : Γ(C.left, U)) ≤ U :=

    letI h_mod_pi₂ : Module R (∀ i, Z₂ i) := Pi.module _ _ _

-- Simulate the consumer pattern: instantiate a theorem with letI, then use it
example {k : Type u} [Field k] (R : Type u) [CommRing R] {n : ℕ} (hn : 0 < n) :
    True := by
  -- mock the theorem: it has Pi.module + per-i Module instance baked in
  let myThm : ∀ {ι : Type u} (Z : ι → ModuleCat.{u} k)
      (e : (∏ᶜ Z : ModuleCat.{u} k) ≃ₗ[k] (∀ i, Z i)),
      letI perI : ∀ i, Module R (Z i) := fun _ => RingHom.toModule (RingHom.id R)
      letI hmod : Module R (∀ i, Z i) := Pi.module _ _ _
      ∀ (r : R) (y : ∀ i, Z i),
        e (e.symm (r • y)) = r • e (e.symm y) := by
    intro ι Z e r y
    rw [LinearEquiv.apply_symm_apply, LinearEquiv.apply_symm_apply]
  -- Now use it: simulate the local context
  let ι : Type u := PUnit
  let Z : ι → ModuleCat.{u} k := fun _ => ModuleCat.of k k
  let e := (ModuleCat.piIsoPi Z).toLinearEquiv
  -- mirror the let-bindings from the theorem
  letI perI_local : ∀ i, Module R (Z i) := fun _ => RingHom.toModule (RingHom.id R)
  letI hmod_local : Module R (∀ i, Z i) := Pi.module _ _ _
  have key := myThm Z e
  -- We expect: key : ∀ r y, e (e.symm (r • y)) = r • e (e.symm y)
  -- and we can use key r y in our local context
  have x : ∀ i, Z i := fun _ => 0
  have r : R := 0
  have eq1 := key r x
  trivial

# Refactor Report

## Slug
basicopencech-specialize-pi-smul

## Status
COMPLETE

## Directive

### Problem (soundness)

The previous iter-087 refactor extracted `cechCofaceMap_pi_smul` as a top-level
theorem with **universally-quantified abstract signature** over
`(Z₁, Z₂, e₁, e₂, scK₀_f, h_mod_pi₁, h_mod_pi₂, R)`. The conclusion
`e₂ (scK₀_f.hom (e₁.symm (r • y))) = r • e₂ (scK₀_f.hom (e₁.symm y))` is
mathematically FALSE for arbitrary `scK₀_f`. Combined with the `sorry` body,
this is functionally a false axiom. Per the project's soundness rule, no
helper with a universally-false signature may be introduced even with `sorry`.

### Changes

1. Replace abstract `cechCofaceMap_pi_smul` (L466–L481) with a concrete one
   specialized to the project's Čech-cochain context (explicit args
   `hU : IsAffineOpen U`, `s₀ : Finset Γ(C.left, U)`, `hn : 0 < n`).
2. Update call site at L1069–L1070 to `cechCofaceMap_pi_smul hU s₀ hn`.
3. Preserve `presheafMap_restrict_collapse` byte-for-byte.
4. Preserve everything else byte-for-byte.
5. Duplicate the `letI perI_i / h_mod_pi_i` block inside the new theorem (the
   `Z_i`/`e_i`/`h_mod_pi_i` quantities must be reproduced to state the
   conclusion).

## Changes Made

### File: `AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/BasicOpenCech.lean`

- **What:** Replaced the abstract `cechCofaceMap_pi_smul` theorem (former
  L466–L481) with a concrete version. The new statement (L455–L495) takes
  `hU : IsAffineOpen U`, `s₀ : Finset Γ(C.left, U)`, `hn : 0 < n` as explicit
  arguments and internally `let`-binds the Čech-cochain quantities
  `R := Γ(C.left, U)`, `K₀ := cechCochain C (toModuleKSheaf C) (basicOpenCover ↑s₀)`,
  `scK₀ := HomologicalComplex.sc K₀ n`, `Z₁`/`Z₂` (the indexed families), and
  `e₁`/`e₂` (the `ModuleCat.piIsoPi`-based linear equivalences). The block
  `letI perI₁ / h_mod_pi₁ / perI₂ / h_mod_pi₂` reproduces the per-i R-module
  structures and the `Pi.module` derivations using the same definitions as
  the inline let-block inside `basicOpenCover_isCechAcyclicCover_toModuleKSheaf`.
  The body remains `sorry` (active iter-088+ prover target).
- **Why:** The previous abstract signature was mathematically false for
  arbitrary `scK₀_f`. Specializing to the Čech-cochain context makes the
  conclusion provable in principle (the Čech differential is an alternating
  sum of presheaf-restriction maps, each R-linear — see
  `presheafMap_restrict_collapse`). This honors the project's soundness rule
  forbidding universally-false signatures.
- **Cascading:** Updated the call site at L1084 from
  `cechCofaceMap_pi_smul (R := R) e₁ e₂ h_mod_pi₁ h_mod_pi₂ scK₀.f` to
  `cechCofaceMap_pi_smul hU s₀ hn`. The local `h_diff_pi_smul_f` continues
  to have the same equational type after let-unfolding, so the downstream
  `h_diff_pi_smul_f r (e₁ x)` rewrite in `f_R.map_smul'` (L1112) continues to
  type-check by definitional equality of the let-bound `R`/`scK₀`/`Z_i`/`e_i`/`h_mod_pi_i`.

- **What (preserved):** `presheafMap_restrict_collapse` at L412–L434 is
  byte-for-byte unchanged. The inline `let`/`letI` block inside
  `basicOpenCover_isCechAcyclicCover_toModuleKSheaf`
  (`Z₁/Z₂/Z₃/e₁/e₂/e₃/perI₁/perI₂/perI₃/h_mod_pi₁/h_mod_pi₂/h_mod_pi₃/h_mod_X₁/h_mod_X₂/h_mod_X₃`)
  at L980–L1069 (post-edit numbering) is byte-for-byte unchanged. The
  `f_R`/`g_R`/`h_loc_X_i`/`h_loc_exact` blocks below are also unchanged.

## New Sorries Introduced

No net new sorries. The previous abstract `cechCofaceMap_pi_smul` carried a
sorry; the new concrete version carries one sorry at the corresponding
location. Sorry count stays at **6** in this file.

- `AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/BasicOpenCech.lean:495` — body of the new
  concrete `cechCofaceMap_pi_smul`. Same as the previous abstract version,
  but now mathematically true (specialized to the Čech-cochain context).
  The iter-088+ prover closes this body.

## Compilation Status

- `AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/BasicOpenCech.lean`:
  **Cannot directly verify in this environment.** Project-level `lake build`
  fails with cascading `permission denied (error code: 13)` errors because
  the package directories under `.lake/packages/` are owned by `root` (not
  `archon`), preventing lake from creating the per-package `.lake` build
  state subdirectories. This is a pre-existing environmental issue unrelated
  to this refactor. The LSP MCP `lean_diagnostic_messages` returns
  `success: false` with empty items on every project file (not just this
  one), confirming the LSP cannot bootstrap due to the same underlying
  permission problem.

  **Indirect verification performed:**
  1. The new theorem's statement structure (let + letI bindings in type
     position, `fun i => by ...` tactic bodies for `perI₁`/`perI₂`,
     `Pi.module _ _ _` for `h_mod_pi₁`/`h_mod_pi₂`, ∀-quantified conclusion)
     was validated standalone via `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_run_code` —
     compiles cleanly with `sorry` body (`success: true`, no diagnostics).
  2. The application/instance-matching pattern (applying a theorem whose
     conclusion contains let-bound module instances at a call site with
     matching local let-bound instances, then using the application term in
     a `rw`) was validated standalone — compiles cleanly.
  3. The replaced theorem differs from the previous abstract version only
     in the *shape* of how `R`, `e₁`, `e₂`, `h_mod_pi₁`, `h_mod_pi₂`,
     `scK₀.f` are reached (let-bound from `hU/s₀/hn` rather than supplied as
     explicit arguments). The conclusion's underlying *expression* after
     let-unfolding is byte-for-byte the same as the previous abstract
     version specialized to the call-site arguments. Hence the downstream
     `f_R.map_smul'` proof (which only references `h_diff_pi_smul_f r (e₁ x)`
     and standard `LinearEquiv` lemmas) continues to type-check.

  Once the environmental permission issue is resolved (a follow-up
  `chown -R archon:archon .lake/packages/`), running `lake build
  AlgebraicJacobian.Cohomology.BasicOpenCech` is expected to succeed with
  the same 6-sorry footprint as iter-086 (one of which is the new
  concrete `cechCofaceMap_pi_smul`).

## Notes for Plan Agent

- **Soundness restored.** The new concrete `cechCofaceMap_pi_smul` cannot be
  used as a false-axiom bypass at unrelated call sites: applying it requires
  a `Finset Γ(C.left, U)` and `IsAffineOpen U`, and its conclusion is
  specifically about `(HomologicalComplex.sc (cechCochain C (toModuleKSheaf C)
  (basicOpenCover ↑s₀)) n).f` — not arbitrary morphisms of products of
  module-cat objects. The mathematical claim (Čech differential is R-linear)
  is true.

- **Letting `perI`/`h_mod_pi` inside the conclusion type works correctly.**
  The standalone test (Test 2 in indirect verification) confirmed that
  `letI` in the type ascription registers the instance for downstream
  resolution within the conclusion and produces an applied term whose type
  reduces correctly at consumer sites. The risk that the theorem's
  `h_mod_pi₁` instance differs from the call site's `h_mod_pi₁` is mitigated
  by definitional equality: both are constructed via byte-identical
  `letI perI := fun i => by apply RingHom.toModule; ...` followed by
  `letI h_mod_pi := Pi.module _ _ _` chains.

- **File line count.** Original (pre-refactor): 1154 LOC. Post-refactor:
  1168 LOC (+14 LOC). The directive estimated +75 LOC because it suggested
  reproducing the *full* letI block (Z₁/Z₂/Z₃/e₁/e₂/e₃/perI₁/perI₂/perI₃/...). I only reproduced the strict subset needed to state the theorem's
  conclusion (Z₁/Z₂/e₁/e₂/perI₁/perI₂/h_mod_pi₁/h_mod_pi₂). The Z₃/h_mod_X_i
  quantities are not referenced in the theorem's conclusion, so they do not
  need to be reproduced.

- **Unused-argument warning may fire on `hU` and `hn`.** Both are accepted
  per the directive's spec but neither is currently referenced in the
  theorem body (the body is `sorry`). The iter-088+ prover filling the body
  will use `hU` (for affine-locality arguments) and `hn` (for positive-degree
  reductions). Lean's `linter.unusedVariables` typically warns but does not
  error on this.

- **Environmental permission issue.** Verified that
  `.lake/packages/*` are owned by `root`, blocking `lake build`. This is a
  pre-existing condition (not caused by this refactor) and affects every
  Lean-build operation in this directory tree. Consider asking the user to
  run `sudo chown -R archon:archon /home/archon/Lean_tests/AlgebraicJacobian/.lake/packages` once.
